subsection.title: Meaning
subsection.p: A pullback rewrites a form in new parameters.
If F:\text{parameter space}\to\text{physical space}, then \boxed{F^*\omega} is the form \omega written in the parameter variables.
Pullbacks are the form version of substitution.

subsection.title: Pullback along a curve
Pullback over a parametrized surface
Flux form pullback
subsection.p: Let \Phi_{\mathbf F} = P\,dy\wedge dz + Q\,dz\wedge dx + R\,dx\wedge dy. Then \boxed{\mathbf r^*\Phi_{\mathbf F} = \mathbf F(\mathbf r(u,v)) \cdot (\mathbf r_u\times\mathbf r_v) \,du\wedge dv.}
Thus \boxed{\iint_S \Phi_{\mathbf F} = \iint_D \mathbf F(\mathbf r(u,v)) \cdot (\mathbf r_u\times\mathbf r_v) \,du\,dv.}

subsection.title: Pullback under a coordinate change
Polar pullback
Cylindrical pullback
Spherical pullback
subsection.p: For x=\rho\sin\phi\cos\theta, y=\rho\sin\phi\sin\theta, z=\rho\cos\phi, \boxed{dx\wedge dy\wedge dz = \rho^2\sin\phi\, d\rho\wedge d\phi\wedge d\theta.}

warning.p: Ordinary change of variables uses absolute values for positive area and volume. Differential forms keep orientation signs.
